title: Subsets
code: for (int i = start; i < nums.Length; i++)

frames.caption: For each element decide include or exclude; recursing forward with start=i+1 visits each of the 2^n combinations once. We record at EVERY node, not just leaves.
frames.note: The subsets decision tree branches into include/exclude for each of the n elements, so it has 2^n leaves and 2^n total subsets. Backtracking walks this tree depth-first: choose, explore, un-choose. The whole run costs O(n * 2^n) — there are 2^n subsets and copying each costs up to O(n).

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=0). Record subset [] (subset #1).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the single most common backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=0: CHOOSE nums[0]=1 -> path becomes [1]. Explore Backtrack(1).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only ever look forward, so each element is decided exactly once and no subset is generated twice — that is why no used[] array is needed here.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=1). Record subset [1] (subset #2).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the single most common backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=1: CHOOSE nums[1]=2 -> path becomes [1,2]. Explore Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only ever look forward, so each element is decided exactly once and no subset is generated twice — that is why no used[] array is needed here.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=2). Record subset [1,2] (subset #3).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the single most common backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=2: CHOOSE nums[2]=3 -> path becomes [1,2,3]. Explore Backtrack(3).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only ever look forward, so each element is decided exactly once and no subset is generated twice — that is why no used[] array is needed here.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=3). Record subset [1,2,3] (subset #4).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the single most common backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=2, i=2: UN-CHOOSE 3 -> path is [1,2] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's choices. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Back at start=1, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path is [1] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's choices. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Done: 8 subsets = 2^3. The recursion tree had 2^3 leaves, one per include/exclude combination.
frames.note: There are exactly 2^n subsets because each element independently is in or out — n binary choices. Pruning cannot make this sub-exponential: the OUTPUT is exponential, so emitting all of it is inherently Omega(2^n). Space (excluding output) is just O(n) for the recursion stack, since only one path is held at a time.
